title: Innovation and capital
abstract: Using a regime change in the commercialization of university innovation in 1980 that strongly increased university incentives to patent and license discoveries, we document that an increase in the supply of commercializable innovation attracts venture capital investment to the region. The Bayh-Dole Act shifted ownership of intellectual property stemming from federally-funded research from the federal government to universities, spurring technology transfer into the local area. Because universities have different technological strengths, each local area surrounding a university experienced an increase after 1980 in commercializable innovation relevant to particular sets of industries which differed widely across university counties. Comparing industries within a county that were more versus less related to the local university's innovative strengths, we show that venture capital dollars after 1980 flowed systematically towards geographic areas and industries affected most by the sudden influx of commercializable innovation from universities. These results persist even when controlling for ex ante geographic and industry distributions of corporate patenting and prior venture financing. The findings support the notion that increased supply of commercializable innovation serves to draw private capital investment to a region.

author.Daniel C. Fehder: University of Southern California, United States
author.Naomi Hausman: The Hebrew University of Jerusalem, Israel
author.Yael V. Hochberg*: Rice University & NBER, 6100 Main St. MS-531, Houston, TX 77005, United States

article.1. Introduction: Venture capital is a key source of financing for the commercialization of new innovation. The geographic distribution of venture capital and other forms of investment for innovation-driven entrepreneurship, however, is highly concentrated. In 2019, Silicon Valley firms received 39 % of all U.S. venture capital (VC) allocations; the top three cities received 60 % of all VC funding deployed; and the top five received 69 % (PwC MoneyTree, 2019). Lack of access to capital to commercialize innovation is a frequently cited reason for some regions’ less well-functioning innovation ecosystems, and it has been one of the most frequently targeted areas for policy intervention (Lerner, 2009). To encourage ecosystem formation, policy makers have often focused—with mixed success (Lerner, 2009)—on interventions designed to provide seed capital in a region or to attract venture capital funds to a region, often in the form of tax breaks for early-stage investment (Denes et al., 2023) or the formation of local government-backed funds. In this paper, we explore an alternative possible focus for policy initiatives, namely, interventions designed to increase the supply of commercializable innovation—which provide the investment opportunities that a capital intervention on its own does not supply and which in turn may draw private investment to the region. We show that VC is drawn to and deployed in response to positive shocks to commercializable innovative activity in a region: increases in the supply of commercializable innovation draw capital that serves as a critical component in the creation of successful entrepreneurial clusters.Identifying the relationship between the supply of commercializable innovation and private capital investment, however, is challenging. To shed light on how supply of commercializable innovation may attract investment capital, we take advantage of a shock to the production and accessibility of commercializable innovation in the vicinity of research universities: the Bayh-Dole Act of 1980. U.S. research universities depend heavily on federal funding for their research (Babina et al., 2023; Jacob and Lefgren, 2011). Inventions and associated intellectual property resulting from such research were historically assigned to the federal government, and most federally-funded research was never patented or commercialized. The Bayh-Dole Act allowed universities to hold property rights to innovations developed at their institutions using federal research funding and provided strong incentives for universities to engage in patenting and licensing activity. As a result, it led to the development of significant infrastructure for technology transfer, which made university innovation become significantly more accessible to industry (Henderson et al., 1998; Sampat et al., 2003). Importantly, because universities differ in research strengths, the Bayh-Dole Act provides a shock to the spread of commercializable innovation that varies not only across geographic areas, but also—depending on the pre-Act research strengths of the nearby universities—across technologies within a geographic area. Our empirical approach uses this within-area variation in the industries related to university research strengths to measure the causal impact of the Bayh-Dole Act's shock to the accessibility of commercializable innovation on venture capital investment, holding other geographic factors constant.To build intuition for the strategy, consider the different pre-1980 innovative strengths of two strong research universities: the University of Texas at Austin (UT) and Johns Hopkins University (JHU). When the Bayh-Dole Act was passed, UT had a top electrical and computer engineering department, but was less strong in biosciences. In contrast, JHU specialized in biosciences research but not in computer engineering. We can thus identify the specific industries in each geographic area that are most likely to benefit from the Bayh-Dole Act's shock to the accessibility of commercializable university innovation. In the Austin, TX area, we would expect the effects of the Bayh-Dole Act to be larger in local electronic and computer-related industries than in pharmaceutical or bioscience-related industries, while in Baltimore, MD, the area surrounding JHU, we would expect the reverse. The nature of this shock allows us to identify the effects of commercializable innovation on capital flows to regions, as we can hold a geographic area fixed, and identify effects off of cross-industry differences in investment capital flows, based on the intensity of field-specific patenting from the nearby university. Our analysis further tightens identification by saturating regressions with fixed effects, including controls for nationwide changes in the flow of investment dollars to particular industries, such that our estimates cannot simply reflect sectoral trends.We focus on VC investment in the region, as it is a primary source of private capital investment in innovation-driven ventures and represents one of the key elements of the financing ecosystem for such ventures. Importantly, the VC activity we measure is venture investment in the local ecosystem generally, not merely in university innovation. The Bayh-Dole Act unleashed a wave of commercializable university innovation into the local economy, which stimulated increased corporate innovation in related industries, and drew VC investment in university counties into new ventures, both directly and indirectly connected to the local university.Our empirical approach further takes advantage of a concurrent increase in fund flows to venture capital firms brought about by three policy changes. The first was the 1979 clarification to the Employee Retirement Income Security Act (ERISA) Prudent Man Rule, which allowed pension funds to invest in higher-risk assets. Quickly following it were the 1980 Small Business Act, which redefined VC fund managers as business development companies, and the 1980 ERISA Safe Harbor regulation, which sanctioned limited partnerships—the dominant organizational form in the VC industry. These regulatory changes resulted in large increases in committed capital to VC firms in the early 1980s—capital which then needed to be deployed across investment opportunities in the U.S. Our findings indicate that these funds were disproportionately deployed in university areas and in the industries most related to their innovative output, even when controlling for the pre-existing distributions of VC investment and corporate patenting. In fact, the locations with the greatest VC growth from 1980 to 1990, shown in Fig. 1 (Panel (d)), so closely resemble the locations of pre-1980 university patenting (Panel (b)) that the attraction of ex-post VC to ex-ante university patenting is apparent, visually, even before examining the full cross-industry variation in each location.Download: Download high-res image (1MB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 1. The Bayh-Dole Act was passed in December 1980.We show empirically that the Bayh-Dole Act-induced shock to the supply of commercializable innovation in the vicinity of top research universities leads to the increased flow of VC funds to university regions over non-university regions, and, specifically, to the industries in each university region that are most closely related to the local universities’ ex-ante technological strengths. We find an increase of $109,000 in VC funds after the Bayh-Dole Act per county-industry, per standard deviation increase in our university “innovation index” measure, or $45,000 per county-industry per citation to a university patent. This effect amounts to approximately $3.9 million additional VC investment per county after the Bayh-Dole Act, while the average university county saw a total increase of $23.4 million in VC funding over the 20 years surrounding both the Bayh-Dole Act and the regulatory changes that increased the supply of VC funding. Simply put, the Bayh-Dole Act shock to the supply of commercializable university innovation appears to account for more than 16 % of the increase in VC funding to these locations. The effect on VC flows is magnified in university areas that received the most federal research funding before the Bayh-Dole Act, further supporting the role of the policy change in releasing patented innovative output that in turn draws private capital investment, and underscores the importance of federal research funding to innovative hubs.1While we focus on measuring the effect of a plausibly exogenous shock to commercializable innovation from universities on VC investment in a region, we also provide evidence of the Bayh-Dole Act's effects on broader measures of interest to policy makers, such as corporate innovation and high growth entrepreneurial activity writ large. We find that, in addition to predicting VC disbursements, ex-ante (pre-1980) university patenting activity (county-industry) also predicts the areas and industries of post-Bayh-Dole Act corporate patenting, even controlling for prior corporate innovation activity. This finding is consistent with the findings documented in Gross and Sampat (2023) that government research funding has extremely persistent effects on the clustering of corporate innovation. Our findings are consistent with VC investment playing a role in this process, and they support the notion that venture capitalists are able to find and fund the best new investment opportunities as they emerge from university ecosystems. Using data from Guzman and Stern (2020) on business registrants and their eventual successful exit events, we further show (i) that ex-ante commercializable university innovation is a strong predictor of subsequent high growth entrepreneurial entry across areas; and (ii) that controlling for contemporary VC investment nearly eliminates this effect—in other words, VCs identify precisely the high growth entrepreneurs launched by cutting edge university ideas. This new evidence suggests a mechanism through which commercializable university innovation attracts VC: the entry of high growth entrepreneurs into university areas and industries related to those universities’ research strengths.Our findings offer a number of distinct contributions to the literature. Disentangling the complex two-way relationship between innovation and capital has long challenged scholars of entrepreneurial finance. This paper contributes to this debate by bringing a new identification approach utilizing a national policy change that created an influx of commercializable university innovation into industry, combined with cross-area and industry variation in the availability of relevant technologies that result from the policy change.Second, we provide policy-relevant evidence suggesting that the encouragement of commercializable innovation activity can draw capital to a region seeking to develop an entrepreneurial cluster. Recognizing the strong link between entrepreneurial activity and economic growth (Davis et al., 1998; Davis and Haltiwanger, 1992; Decker et al., 2014; Fairlie et al., 2019; Guzman and Stern, 2020; Haltiwanger et al., 2013; Jovanovic and MacDonald, 1994), policy makers often seek ways to stimulate and support entrepreneurial activity in their local areas (Lerner, 2009). Entrepreneurs are often considered to be drivers of urban growth in general, and of innovation-driven growth in particular (Agrawal et al., 2010; Glaeser et al., 2015, 2010; Glaeser and Kerr, 2009; Hausman, 2020; Shane, 2004). Our findings suggest that one additional tool in the policy maker's toolbox is to engage in policies and interventions that encourage commercializable innovation activity, while allowing commercialization to be supported by private capital that then flows into the market in its wake. Such interventions designed to promote the supply of commercializable innovation may be more cost effective than creating a government-backed fund or offering subsidies to VCs to enter a region.Third, our paper contributes to a large literature on the sources of agglomeration economies that generate clusters of industrial activity and healthy entrepreneurial ecosystems. Knowledge spillovers, input-output relationships (supply chains), and labor pooling have long been thought to drive the co-location of firms (Ellison et al., 2010; Greenstone et al., 2010; Marshall, 1890). Substantial theoretical and empirical evidence supports the notion that innovation and entrepreneurship emerge in large part from the mixing of ideas in localities (Agrawal et al., 2008; Duranton and Puga, 2001; Glaeser et al., 1992) and that use of innovation is disproportionately local (Jaffe et al., 1993; Kerr, 2010). Our paper illustrates the importance of input-output relationships in facilitating innovative clusters around universities, as VC is a critical input for many entrepreneurs and is allocated disproportionately locally, likely due to the ease of monitoring and advising proximate companies (Chen et al., 2010; Lerner, 1995).Finally, our findings contribute to the growing literature on the influence of universities and government-funded research on economic growth. Universities may generate positive spillovers for their local economies via skilled workers (Andrews, 2023; Cantoni and Yuchtman, 2014; Dittmar and Meisenzahl, 2022; Moretti, 2004), university spending (Kantor and Whalley, 2014), or knowledge spillovers (Dittmar and Meisenzahl, 2022; Hausman, 2022). Our paper demonstrates an additional mechanism through which universities can generate clustered growth: by attracting venture capital to finance the university-related frontier innovation of local entrepreneurs.
article.2. Government-funded research and the Ba: Since the Second World War, the U.S. federal government has been the country's largest source of funding for research and development. Most university-based research is funded through grants. Grants provide money, equipment, or both to eligible researchers to carry out approved projects or activities. The grantee is responsible for conducting the project activities, reporting on progress, and preparing the results for publication. Federal research funding has a large impact on the productivity of academic researchers (Jacob and Lefgren, 2011) and on the direction of their research (Myers, 2020). A substantial literature has explored the effects of such government funding for research on the innovation economy (see e.g. Gruber and Johnson, 2019). Federal funding has been found to spur the development of private-sector patents (Azoulay et al., 2019; Cockburn and Henderson, 1998; Jaffe, 1989), and cuts to federal funding have been shown shift university research funding from federal to private sources, leading to less accessible innovation outputs that are often appropriated by the private funders (Babina et al., 2023).Importantly, most of the technologies developed at universities, whether funded by the government or by private funders, are embryonic at best, with the human capital of the academic inventors tied closely to the value of the invention as the inventor's cooperation remains crucial to its commercialization. Academic researchers are more focused on novel, basic research that pushes forward the frontier of knowledge, and they are likely to shift their attention to competing research streams after initial invention, creating a contracting problem for commercializing academic innovation (Aghion et al., 2008). Jensen and Thursby (2001) emphasize the embryonic state of the technologies licensed by universities. Commercialization of such research is in large part undertaken by outside entities, who invest in the translational and applied research necessary to derisk the technology and bring it to market in commercializable form, and who license the inventions for this purpose, typically in the form of patents or patent portfolios.2 These outside entities include large public and private companies and startups, both VC- and non-VC-backed. Federally-funded university research also affects economic activity through local knowledge spillovers, educated labor supply, and attraction of population to the local area (Andrews, 2023).Until the 1980s, many American universities were reluctant to engage directly in commercialization of research. Typically, this avoidance was justified by arguments that patenting and licensing of technology removed knowledge from the public domain. Many universities feared that commercialization compromised the academy's commitment to open science, or that profit motives might undermine the purity of the scientific endeavor. Sampat (2006), for example, describes Columbia University's reasoning that patenting activity “is not deemed within the sphere of the University's scholarly objectives.” Many top research universities went so far as to explicitly forbid the patenting of biomedical research (Mowery and Sampat, 2001).3The legal regime further disincentivized commercialization of university research. With the WWII explosion of federal funding for research in universities, research organizations, and private companies, debates over the ownership of inventions resulting from such funding were fierce. Initially, most federal funding contracts stipulated that the government would retain title to any patents that resulted from its funding, under the presumption that the discoveries from publicly-funded research should belong to the public. Each federal agency determined its own patent policy, with little uniformity across agencies (Gross and Sampat, 2024). Typically, rights to royalties or other income from intellectual property developed at universities using federally-funded research—which constituted the majority of total activity at U.S. universities—were held by the federal government.4 While researchers could patent their innovations, they could not keep any royalties from licensing such patents unless they negotiated a special Institutional Patent Agreement (IPA) with the granting agency—a process typically involving lawyers, lengthy negotiations, drafts of agreements, and significant other administrative burden. While government title purported to promote a regime of “open science,” in practice, most innovations in the public domain were not publicized or commercialized, and only 5 % of the 28,000 patents owned by the federal government in 1976 were licensed.5 Universities and innovators themselves—those most familiar with the technologies to be transferred—had little incentive to promote their ideas to industry.The debate leading up to the passage of the Bayh-Dole Act focused primarily on the benefits of public knowledge and “open science” versus the benefits of incentives to innovate and commercialize research. One view held that federally financed innovations should be kept in the public domain to maximize potential spillovers. Countering it were those worried that private enterprise would not fully invest in discovery and commercialization without stronger intellectual property protection that allowed them to benefit from the innovation developed in the course of contract R&D, and that incentives were needed to encourage the marketing of federally-funded innovations to corporate developers who might choose to commercialize them. Concerns regarding the U.S.’s ability to maintain economic competitiveness added further pressure to provide incentives for innovation.In December 1980, Congress passed the Bayh-Dole Act. The Act standardized patent policy across granting agencies and reversed the presumption of federal title to inventions developed under federally funded research. The Bayh-Dole Act gave universities the right to patent, own rights to, and keep royalty revenues from innovations developed using federal research funding. As quid pro quo, the Act also required universities to actively promote the inventions’ commercialization, grant the federal government a non-exclusive license, and share any royalties with the inventor. In 1984, Congress further passed the Trademark Clarification Act, which removed restrictions on the types of inventions universities could own, and on the transfer of property rights to other parties. Taken together, these laws significantly strengthened the incentives of universities and faculty to produce, patent, and commercialize innovation.6The Bayh-Dole Act required universities to establish mechanisms for disclosure of new inventions funded by federal research funds, report inventions to the sponsoring agency, promote the commercialization of technologies resulting from federally funded research, and share income from licensing of such inventions with the academic inventors. Universities and faculty appear to have responded to these new incentives, opening Technology Transfer Offices (TTOs, also sometimes referred to as Technology Licensing Office, or TLOs) at increasing rates in the 1980s and early 1990s (Feldman et al., 2002).7 TTOs were typically tasked with collecting invention disclosures from university researchers, filing for patent protection, forming industry partnerships, and licensing technologies to outside entities. Along with the creation of technology transfer offices, universities created new policies around the disclosure of intellectual property. For example, in 1981, in response to the Bayh-Dole Act, Columbia University mandated the disclosure of all potential inventions created within university research facilities (Mowery et al., 2015). TTOs experimented with different types of licensing options for their intellectual property. Areas of experimentation included contracts with varying levels of exclusivity and royalty structures, and licensing to spin-out startups in exchange for equity stakes in the new venture (Feldman et al., 2002). As controversies and disputes arose, such as those related to Harvard's exclusive licensing of “OncoMouse” intellectual property to DuPont, academic researchers and administrators worked to understand how to adjudicate between commercial opportunities and the norms of open science (Murray, 2010). As universities increasingly pursued licensing opportunities, many were able to enter into royalty-generating contracts, and some were able to generate substantial revenue, fostering the idea that technology licensing could serve as a significant source of funds to support a university's mission (Colyvas et al., 2002; Sampat, 2006). In addition, commercializing university inventions became a source of prestige.Patenting from universities rose correspondingly (Henderson et al., 1998), with the sharpest increase beginning in the late 1980s, as university infrastructure adjusted to handle faculty disclosures, patent applications, and licensing on a large scale (Thursby et al., 2001). University patenting grew substantially over time, from 294 patents granted to universities in 1976 to over 2000 1990 (Fig. 2). Patenting also became more pervasive across universities; while only 132 universities had patented prior to the passage of the Bayh-Dole Act, 232 universities had been granted at least one patent by 1995 and 340 by 2006.8 Although it is difficult to say whether faculty responded by producing more innovation after the Bayh-Dole Act, Lach and Schankerman (2008) provide evidence suggesting that faculty responded to stronger royalty incentives by producing higher quality inventions.9Download: Download high-res image (160KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 2. University patenting, 1970–1990.Notes: Figure shows annual number of patents produced by research universities and hospitals, by year, from the NBER Patent Database.Overall, the Bayh-Dole Act, with its effects on both university culture and incentives, resulted in a significant shift in the relationship between universities and industry. While prior to the Act, large scale technology and idea transfer from universities was nearly impossible to achieve, the Act strengthened property rights, standardized these rights across granting agencies, and provided significant economic incentives to both researchers and university administrations to engage in patenting and licensing activity.
article.3. Data and sample: 3.1. Patent dataWe use patent data for several purposes, including to measure (i) universities’ pre-Bayh-Dole Act innovative strengths, (ii) corporate innovation outcomes, and (iii) the pre-Bayh-Dole Act distribution of corporate innovative activity across industries and areas as a counterfactual target for VC flows. The National Bureau of Economic Research Patent Data Project provides a compiled version of publicly available data from the United States Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO) on utility patents granted between 1976 and 2006 (Hall et al., 2001).10 The data contain year of patent application and grant, assignee, assignee location, patent technology class, and forward and backward citations. Assignees (patent owners) may be individuals, U.S. or foreign corporations, U.S. or foreign governments, hospitals, or universities. We use the subset of patents assigned to universities and university-affiliated hospitals to generate our sample of innovating universities, identify the research fields in which each university is highly innovative, and connect these research fields to the industries that may use a particular research field's innovations.11 Additionally, we use data on the distribution of pre-1980 corporate patents to help distinguish the effects of the Bayh-Dole Act from that of the 1979 ERISA Prudent Man Rule, which may have increased VC funding to areas with strong corporate innovation.Because universities often chose not to patent inventions pre-Bayh-Dole Act, particularly those that resulted from federally funded research, we augment the NBER patent data in the pre-period using data from Gross and Sampat (2023) that consists of all patents that are based on federally-funded research, regardless of to whom they are assigned. Within this sample, we identify patents that have at least one inventor with a university affiliation, as described in Appendix A. This results in an additional 277 patents attributable to a university in the pre-period, which are added to the sample from the NBER patent data.3.2. University innovation indexTo construct a measure of university innovation and its relationship to specific industries, we follow Hausman (2022) and begin with patents produced by the universities and hospitals in our sample and then expand the sample of patents to include federally-funded inventions by university researchers regardless of assignee (See Appendix A). Each patent linked to each university is assigned a technology class by the USPTO. On their own, these technology classes are difficult for a non-specialist to connect to a specific industry. We follow Kerr (2008) and use a probabilistic concordance constructed by experienced practitioners that weights each 3-digit SIC industry in terms of the probability it will use a patent given its technology classification.12 The weights, w, sum to one across SIC-3 industries for each USPTO technology class. The university-industry-specific index is thus a sum of the weights across a university's patents and within an SIC-3 industry. For counties that contain multiple universities, the index is then summed across universities. In Appendix A we provide a more detailed explanation of the calculation process including Fig. A1 that presents a stylized example of how the index is constructed, following the equation:indexci=∑u∈c∑nwinpunwhere p is the number of citation-weighted patents granted to university u in technology class n, and w is the frequency of use weight for patent class n in industry i. The measure is then standardized to be mean zero, standard deviation one.Recent research (e.g., Hochberg et al., 2024) has drawn attention to the presence of potential biases in forward citations to patents based on the gender of inventors. For robustness, we also calculate a university innovation index that does not citation weight. Analysis using the unweighted index can be found in Appendix B.3.3. NSF federal research funding to universitiesData on federal research funding to universities spanning the period 1963–2007 come from the National Science Foundation's publicly available survey on Federal Science and Engineering Support to Universities, Colleges, and Non-Profit Institutions. The data contain funding amounts by government agency, university campus, category of spending, and year. We use average annual funding amounts to the top universities in our sample in the five years leading up to the Bayh-Dole Act, 1976–1980, to approximate the scale of ex-ante research explicitly subject to changes brought by the Bayh-Dole Act, although in practice these changes affected most university research because of the infrastructure required for commercialization.To provide a sense of the funding magnitudes, in 1980, MIT received $163.2 million in total funds, $26.9 million of which came from the Department of Defense (DOD) and $27.2 million of which came from the National Institutes of Health (NIH). A much less research-intensive university, Montana State University at Bozeman, received considerably fewer federal funds in 1980: $10.6 million total, of which $381,000 originated from DOD, and $346,000 originated from NIH. As with patenting activity, federal funding is highly concentrated among top universities.3.4. University countiesIn our main sample, we consider counties containing top 100 patenting universities to be “university counties;” the top 100 patenting universities reside in only 75 counties. These university counties are considered to be “treated” by the innovation index and the federal funding associated with local universities.13 All other counties are considered untreated. In robustness work, we expand the definition of university county first to include adjacent counties and second to include those within 75 miles of the university. We also show robustness to expanding the definition to include all universities that received at least one patent before passage of the Bayh-Dole Act (notably, the R1 classification for research universities included only 137 universities in 2022.)3.5. VC funding dataOur VC data comes from Thompson VentureXpert at the deal level, with information on funded companies, location, industry, investor, and funds invested. We aggregate these deal level data to the county-SIC3-year level, computing three measures: Total Number of Active VC Investors (Num Investors), Total Number of Deals conducted (Num Deals), and Total Funding Invested (Total Funds). We utilize VC funding as a proxy for private capital investment in innovation-driven enterprises, while noting that VC-backed companies are not the sole channel through which university research is commercialized.3.6. Corporate innovation indexWe measure the pre-Bayh-Dole Act distribution of corporate innovation across geographic areas and industries much as we do for university innovation, using patents produced in the years 1976–1980. The corporate innovation index for each county c and industry i is the weighted sum over technology classes n of citation-weighted corporate patents, cp, in county c and technology class n, where the frequency of use weights w reflect the probability that a patent of each technology class n is used in industry i:corpindexci=∑nwincpcnAs with the university innovation index, the corporate innovation index is standardized to be mean zero, standard deviation one, such that the coefficients are directly comparable.3.7. VC indexTo estimate a plausibly causal effect of VC in certain analyses, we would ideally like to measure the presence of venture capital across geographic areas and industries in a manner that is exogenous to corporate innovation. Measuring pre-ERISA (pre-1979) VC allocations is a first step, since ERISA greatly increased the scale of funds available to VC firms for investment and thus provides useful variation over time. But of course, even before ERISA, VC investments were presumably made where opportunities for useful innovation were greatest – likely where corporate innovation was strong. Because corporate innovation exhibits high autocorrelation over time, measuring ex-ante VC on its own would be endogenous to ex-post corporate innovation. We thus follow Kortum and Lerner (2000) in scaling ex-ante VC in each county and industry by corporate R&D investments in the same locations. By using investment inputs rather than patenting outputs, we aim to control for expected innovation opportunities, given information available at the time.We measure the VC index in county c and industry i as the VC funds invested in that county and industry in the years 1970–1979 divided by county-industry level corporate R&D investment (from Compustat) in the same years:VCindexci=VCciRnDciWe standardize our index to be mean zero and standard deviation one, as with the other indices, and we refer to it as the Ex-Ante VC Index.14 The ability of this measure to predict post-1979 corporate innovation will thus reflect venture capitalists’ ability to identify and invest in the places and industries that would become the strongest in frontier innovation, i.e. as distinct from the areas strongest in established innovation as of 1979.3.8. Corporate patentingImportantly, VC backed startups are not the only vehicle for commercialization of university research. University inventions are licensed by established corporations as well. Using the designator in the NBER patent data that indicates a corporate assignee, we compute an overall measure of citation-weighted corporate patenting and raw count corporate patenting at the county-industry-year level.3.9. High-growth entrepreneurship measuresTo measure high growth entrepreneurship activity by county, we use data provided in 2020 by the Startup Cartography Project (SCP), described in detail in Guzman and Stern (2020). These data are categorized by the business registration year of new firms and offer information on these registrants’ eventual business outcomes. We use four variables calculated by the data providers: Entrants with Eventual Growth Events, Quality-Adjusted Quantity of Entrants, Entrant Quality Index, and Realized-to-Expected Eventual Growth Events.3.10. Final sampleThe sample that results from combining these various data sources contains observations at the county-industry-year level, where industries are measured at the 3-digit SIC code level and the sample years are 1970–1990. Because there are often multiple universities and hospitals in a given county or in nearby counties, their associated treatments (federal funding and the Innovation Index) are summed across universities within treated county-industries. Thus, for example, Middlesex County, MA, is treated by both Harvard and MIT, and a biomedical industry in that county would be treated by the biomedical innovation index that is the sum of those from each school. We assume the Bayh-Dole Act treats only university counties (as defined in Section 3.4) and only in the years after 1980.Table 1, Panel A presents descriptive statistics for the estimation sample at the county-industry-year level. To provide a sense of the state of university counties before the policy changes we study, Panel B presents county-level statistics for university counties prior to 1979. Empirical analysis: To measure the ability of local commercializable innovation activity to attract private capital flows from venture capitalists, we would ideally like to allocate innovation randomly to otherwise similar locations and measure venture capital investment in those locations after the innovation is endowed, relative to before. In reality, of course, innovation is not exogenously endowed or allocated. Instead, we take advantage of a national policy change–the Bayh-Dole Act of 1980–that generates a plausibly exogenous shock to the spread of commercializable innovation from universities and should have different expected effects across geographical areas and industries.4.1. Main resultsTo set the stage, we begin with a basic, relatively weakly-identified comparison of university counties to non-university counties before and after the passage of the Bayh-Dole Act, before turning to our main analysis, which accounts for the differential impact of universities on industries in their region most tied to their innovative activities. If the supply of commercializable innovation serves as a key channel to attract private investment capital from VCs to a region, we would expect the difference between university counties and non-university counties to be larger after the passage of the Bayh-Dole Act, which provides a shock to commercializable innovative output in university counties. Fig. 3 presents the raw averages of VC dollars invested, number of unique active VC investors, and number of VC deals over time for university and non-university counties. As can be seen clearly from the figure, university counties experience a larger increase in all three VC activity variables after 1980, relative to non-university counties. The graphs for the three VC measures show similar pre-treatment trends in university and non-university counties, consistent with VC funding flowing to areas that are “shocked” with more commercializable innovation. To examine this cross-county relationship more formally, we estimate variations of the model:(1)ycit=β0+β1(Iyear>1980*Icuniv_county)+ϑit+θci+ϵcit,where the unit of observation is a county-industry-year. The coefficient β1 captures the differential effect of the Bayh-Dole Act on university-containing versus non-university-containing counties, after relative to before the passage of the law; ϑit are industry X year fixed effects; and θci are county X industry fixed effects. The inclusion of industry X year fixed effects is meant to capture industry increases in venture capital nationwide that are not attributable to university innovation, while county X industry fixed effects account for location-specific industry strengths that are consistent over time. Due to the fixed-effects structure, both lower order terms—the indicator for university-containing county and the indicator for year post 1980—are absorbed in the fixed effects. First, there is a positive and highly significant effect for adjacent counties, though it is, as would be expected, smaller than that for university counties. This geographic decay is expected given the large literature on the extent of localization of knowledge spillovers from innovation (Belenzon and Schankerman, 2012; Hausman, 2022; e.g., Jaffe et al., 1993) and on the localization of VC (Chen et al., 2010). Second, the effect for university counties becomes slightly larger when adjacent counties are not included in the reference group (columns 4–6 versus columns 1–3). These results accord with the literature on the localization of innovation knowledge spillovers and VC investment and its attenuation over distance. Importantly, these cross-county estimates reflect average effects across many university county-industries, most of which do not attract VC at all, and, as a result, their magnitudes may mask larger industry-specific effects in industries closely tied to commercializable university innovation output, as we will see below.While these patterns are consistent with the notion that the Bayh-Dole Act's shock to the accessibility of commercializable university innovation leads to greater flows of private capital into university counties, these tests are relatively weakly identified—university, adjacent, and non-university counties differ on a variety of dimensions that could affect VC outcomes. To fully identify the effects of the Bayh-Dole Act's commercializable innovation shock on VC funding flows, we next take advantage of cross-sectional variation in the industries in each county most likely to be affected by such a shock, given the pre-treatment research expertise of the nearby university, as measured by the University Innovation Index.The expected differential effects of the shock on VC funding deployed in each county-industry are best illustrated by example: at the time of the Bayh-Dole Act's passing, University of Montana, Bozeman, for example, was particularly strong in research in optics and photonics—it has a high innovation index in that industry—and we thus expect to see VC funds flow specifically to that industry in the Bozeman area in the wake of the Act's passage. Similarly, Texas A&M University was particularly strong in innovation in chemical engineering closely related to biotechnology, and we expect more VC fund flows to investments into biotechnology in the College Station area.Formally, we estimate variations on the following equation:(2)VCcit=β0+β1(Iyear>1980*univ_indexci)+ϑit+φct+θci+ϵcit,where the unit of observation is a county-industry-year; the coefficient β1 captures the differential effect of the shock to commercializable university innovation on high innovation index versus low innovation index industries in a given region following the passage of the Bayh-Dole Act, relative to before the passage of the law; ϑit are industry X year fixed effects; φct are county X year fixed effects; and θci are county X industry fixed effects. The inclusion of industry X year fixed effects captures rising VC flows to each industry nationwide, while the county X year fixed effects absorb any cross-industry shocks over time in a given location that are unrelated to university innovation. County X industry fixed effects account for location-specific industry strengths that are constant over time. Lastly, univ_indexci is the standardized university innovation index for county c in industry i, reflecting the extent to which each industry in each location should be affected by the commercializable innovation shock, based on the strength of its relationship to the innovation output of the specific universities in that county. Due to the model's saturation with fixed effects, the main effect of the innovation index is absorbed, as is the indicator for post-1980. First, our main results use a window between 1970–1990, so we examine the robustness of our results to a longer window from 1970–1995 in appendix Table B1 and present the corresponding parallel trends graph in Fig. B1. An increase of 1 % in the innovation index for a single industry in the county yields a 2.7 to 3.9 % increase in investment dollars, investors, and deals. Altogether, the estimates suggest a substantial impact of the supply of new commercializable innovation on the allocation of early-stage investment. As with the robustness tests described above, we show little change in our results to changes in time window (Table B1), in the sample of universities (Table B2), in construction of the university innovation index using patents through 1985 (Table B3), or in construction of the index using raw patent counts (Table B4). In appendix Table B5, we additionally show that our results, while slightly smaller in magnitude, are robust to the exclusion of the top ten university-fields in pre-Bayh-Dole Act patenting intensity by patent class and additionally excluding California and Massachusetts, the two leading regions in venture capital allocation in the pre-Bayh-Dole Act period.4.2. Geographic decayOur main results assume the university “treats” only the county in which it is contained. Next, we explore the geographic decay of knowledge spillovers and the corresponding local nature of effects. Because knowledge spillovers are disproportionately local in nature (Jaffe et al., 1993), we would expect our results to decay with distance, and we have already shown clear geographic decay in the county level analysis of Table 2. We now re-run our better-identified, county-industry analyses with two alternative datasets: one in which we code counties adjacent to university counties as treated and one in which we count as treated all counties within 75 miles of a sample research university. The estimates are as expected: the effect of the Bayh-Dole Act is smaller as we get further from the university (Appendix Table B6). The fact that these estimates decay with distance further supports the importance of local knowledge spillovers—as university-related corporate innovation and entrepreneurship increase more in close proximity to the university—and of localized input-output relationships, as VC flows disproportionately to firms in the university's immediate vicinity. In the absence of disproportionately local knowledge spillovers, university innovation would not draw VC funding to the local area—innovation could spread equally quickly to any region, however far away, and VC investment would follow that knowledge spillover. Our estimates in Appendix Table B6 thus further affirm the important role of local knowledge spillovers in determining where commercialization of innovative activity occurs.4.3. Mechanism test: federal fundingBecause the Bayh-Dole Act technically affected commercializable university innovation produced in the course of federally funded research, universities that received the most federal research funding before the law was passed would have had the most research suddenly opened to commercialization. We can thus provide further support for our identification scheme and mechanism of effects by employing variation in the amount of federal research funding received by universities in the years prior to the Bayh-Dole Act. If federal research funding increases the intensity of treatment, then the VC growth gap between more and less treated industries (as measured by the innovation index) should be greater in counties that received more federal research funding ex-ante. This prediction amounts to estimating an equation analogous to Eq. (2), but in which the term of interest is now a triple interaction between the innovation index, an indicator for years after the Bayh-Dole Act, and average annual federal funding to universities in the county in the years leading up to 1980:(3)VCcit=β0+β1(Iyear>1980*univ_indexci)+β2(avg_Fed_Fund*Iyear>1980*univ_indexci)*+ϑit+φct+θci+ϵcit.β2 is expected to be positive if federal funding magnifies between-industry differences in effects of commercializable university innovation. The fixed effects are identical to those in Eq. Controlling for existing patterns of VC investment and corporate patentingThe passage of the Bayh-Dole Act coincided with other regulatory changes that affected the amount of capital available for VCs to invest more generally. In particular, the Employee Retirement Income Security Act (ERISA)’s 1979 clarification of the “Prudent Man” rule in late 1979 allowed pension funds to invest in higher-risk asset classes. This rule change allowed funds from defined benefit pension funds to be funneled into the VC industry, as a result of which VCs suddenly had considerably more capital available to deploy than they had in prior years. This capital then needed to be invested to generate returns for limited partners. We have already seen that post-Bay-Dole and the ERISA clarifications, VC investment flowed at a higher rate into the university counties and industries associated with their research, particularly for universities with more federally-funded research, relative to non-university counties and non-related industries. An interesting question is whether this increased pool of VC also flowed disproportionately to university counties in the industries most related to university the university's research and invention strengths when compared against other possible options, such as locations and industries with high stocks of ex ante corporate patenting, or locations and industries that had been magnets for VC investment up to that point, given the possibility for spillover effects from such investment that generate future innovation (e.g., Kortum and Lerner, 2000).To explore this, we construct an index measure for pre-1979 VC activity. We follow Kortum and Lerner (2000), and scale VC investment by contemporary corporate R&D expenditures (to control for known innovation opportunities). Our index is calculated at the industry-county level. We additionally calculate an index measure for ex-ante corporate innovation by county and industry that is analogous to the university innovation index but is based on pre-1980 corporate patents rather than university-related patents. We then “horse-race” these innovation indices against the university innovation index in models similar to our main analysis.The estimates are presented in Table 6. Columns (1)-(3) of Table 6 presents estimates from models using our customary set of VC activity outcome variables, horse-racing the university innovation index against the ex ante VC index. Across three VC activity variables, the coefficients on Iyear>1980*univ_indexci are positive and statistically significant, showing that the Bayh-Dole Act had a marked impact on the allocation of VC to university counties in the industries most closely tied to the university's commercializable innovation output even after accounting for the geography-industry distribution of the scaled level of VC allocation before the Act. In two out of the three models, we observe a small and significant association of post 1980 VC activity with pre-period levels of VC allocation, unsurprising given the extreme geographic localization of venture capital (Chen et al., 2010). The magnitude of these coefficients, however, is between 4.75 and 6.3 times lower than the size of estimate of the effect of the university innovation index (coefficients are comparable given the standardization of the index variables). Across all regressions, the difference between the university and VC interaction term coefficients is statistically significant (p-values ranging from 0.053 to 0.03). The estimates suggest that the commercializable university innovation shock from the Bayh-Dole Act leads specifically to disproportionate flow of VC investment activities to the affected industries in university counties even after accounting for pre-existing patterns of VC allocation, supporting the causal channel for the impact of commercializable university innovation output on VC flows. Error bars indicate 95 % confidence intervals.Next, we examine the flow of VC investment to university counties in industries closely related to the university's research versus the flow to county-industries with a large pre-existing stock of corporate innovation. Prior research has shown that federally-funded research in a region can generate clusters that generate substantial innovation, but these clusters can expand in scope beyond the initial research, generating differences in the extent of corporate innovation versus research in universities (Gross and Sampat, 2023). The maps in Fig. 1 suggest that the geographic distribution of corporate innovation prior to 1980 was considerably wider than that of university innovation (Panels (a) and (b)). The industry distribution of established corporate innovation may also have differed somewhat from the distribution of industries affected by commercializable university innovation.Columns (4)-(6) of Table 6 present estimates where we horse-race the university innovation index versus the corporate innovation index. Similar to columns (1)-(3), we observe a positive and statistically significant coefficient on the university innovation index for all three measures of VC activity, controlling for the corporate innovation index interaction. Put differently, even controlling for the effects of the geographic distribution of corporate innovation output pre-shock, VC activity increases post-ERISA precisely where the predicted effects of the Bayh-Dole Act would expect it to be – in university counties, in the industries most closely tied to university innovation output. Interestingly, in contrast, the coefficient on the corporate innovation index interaction does not load significantly in any of the specifications. These patterns lend further support for our interpretation of the Bayh-Dole Act reform as a major channel for determining venture capital allocation in this period. In appendix Table B9, we show that are results are robust to an alternative construction of our university innovation index using unweighted patent counts.In Panel B of Fig. 5, we provide a graphical comparison of the impact university innovation to corporate innovation on subsequent VC investment. The graph is constructed using the same method as described above for Panel A of Fig. 5. We observe strong effects of the Bayh-Dole Act in industry-counties with high levels of university innovation, and only limited and non-statistically significant increases in VC funding in county-industries with high ex ante corporate innovation. The pattern shown in Panel B of Fig. 5 further supports our hypothesized effect of the Bayh-Dole Act on the commercialization of university intellectual property.Lastly, we include all three indices in an expanded regression, and compare the relative flow of VC investment post-Bayh-Dole Act across the three. The estimates from the three-way horse-race model are presented in columns (7)-(9) of Table 6. Overall, the simultaneous estimates of the coefficients on the interactions of post Bayh-Dole with the three indices show little difference from the patterns observed in columns (1) through (6). We observe a positive and statistically significant coefficient on the university innovation index in the post-Bayh-Dole Act period across our three different measures of venture capital allocation. Neither pre-Bayh-Dole Act VC activity location-industry distribution nor pre-Bayh-Dole Act corporate innovation location-industry distribution exhibit a consistently significant relationship with post-Bayh-Dole Act VC activity in the county-industry, while the coefficient on the university innovation index remains large and significant. Overall, the results are consistent with the VC investment post-Bayh-Dole Act flowing disproportionately into university counties in the industries most closely related to university research, consistent with our hypothesis that increased commercializable innovation can draw capital to the region.4.6. Other spillover effects: corporate patenting and high-growth outcomesWe next turn to examining the effects of the shock to university commercializable innovation on other commercialization-related outcomes. While VC is one important channel for commercialization of university innovation, it not the only one. We next estimate models similar to the horse-race models in the previous section, but replace the VC outcomes on the left-hand side with corporate patenting activity, allowing us to shed light on a broader range of innovation spillovers from the Bayh-Dole Act. Table 7 presents the results of our estimation using the citation-weighted number of corporate patents as the dependent variable. In models where each index interaction (university, VC, corporate) is included alone (columns (1) to (3)), only the university innovation index and corporate innovation index load significantly. In column (4), we include interaction terms for all three indices. Here, again, we observe a positive and significant loading on the university innovation index interaction and the corporate innovation index interaction, but not on the VC index interaction. Once we control for both the existing stock of corporate innovation and the shock to commercializable university innovation output, ex ante VC dollars on their own do not exhibit a significant relationship with future innovation. Put differently, these estimates are consistent with the notion that, in the absence of commercializable innovation activity to provide opportunities for investment of funds and corporations, VC availability in a region alone (through a government fund, for example) may not be enough to drive future innovation activity. The results also indicate that the shock to commercializable university innovation led to an increase in (codified) local industrial innovation, amplifying the local increase in supply of commercializable accessible innovation.Table 7. When this measure is included, we observe that contemporary VC dollars invested are not only strongly positively correlated with high growth entrepreneurship, but also drive down the coefficient on the university innovation index. The university innovation effect declines from 0.472 in column (1) to 0.200 in column (3), and from positive and significant in column (4) to statistically indistinguishable from zero in column (6). This result could not occur without a strong positive correlation between university innovation and subsequent VC disbursements. The implication is that commercializable university innovation stimulates high growth entrepreneurship insofar as it attracts of VC investment. Put differently, VC investors are drawn specifically to the frontier innovation stimulated by basic university research, and they manage to identify precisely the entrepreneurial entrants most likely to succeed (or who can be led to success). Logically, VC is even more strongly correlated with the subset of corporate innovation that occurs in new, high growth firms. These results offer a suggestion of a possible virtuous cycle, with innovation output attracting capital that further stimulates innovation, and leading to the emergence of high growth entrepreneurial clusters.
article.5. Conclusion: Economists since Adam Smith have emphasized the importance of entrepreneurs and new business formation to the economy. Understanding the forces underlying the formation of entrepreneurial clusters—particularly of a high growth, innovation-driven nature—is of critical interest to economists and policy makers alike. In this paper, we provide evidence that innovative activity attracts venture capital financing, which in turn may lead to the production of additional innovation in the future.Our findings have several policy implications. First, intellectual property policy that provides incentives for the commercialization of university innovation appears to have positive effects for the local economies nearby. Not only does the new, post-Bayh-Dole Act access to commercializable university innovation increase local agglomeration of related industries (Hausman, 2022), but also, as we show in this paper, the resulting rise in local industrial innovative activity draws venture capital, a crucial input for the high growth entrepreneurs working to develop university related ideas. The role of VC in this next step on the path of ideas from universities to private sector innovation is likely to be vital: VC is present in all highly successful clusters of innovation, and we provide evidence suggestive of its role in university-related high growth entrepreneurship. The Bayh-Dole Act thus released valuable commercializable innovation to university economies after 1980, creating the opportunity for VC dollars—which may otherwise have gone to feed established corporate innovation—to hone in on the frontier innovation being developed around universities.Second, our results inform local development policy, speaking particularly to policy makers seeking to cultivate IDE ecosystems. Because VC funds invest disproportionately locally (Chen et al., 2010), the ability to draw VC to a region to fund innovation clusters is considered essential. We bring new identification to the question of whether local innovation can attract VC, showing that a positive shock to frontier commercializable innovation draws private capital investment to the region. The importance of strong local innovation may be one reason why policy efforts to exogenously provide seed capital or attract venture capital to a region—such as tax breaks for early stage investment and the formation of local government backed funds—have met with mixed success (Denes et al., 2023; Lerner, 2009). The evidence in this paper suggests that spending money on programs to encourage local commercializable innovation may be more productive in developing local ecosystems than spending on programs to create venture funds directly. Specifically, by encouraging formal technology codification and transfer, informal knowledge sharing, and density of skilled workers, policy makers can harness the substantial power universities have to stimulate local high growth entrepreneurial clusters.
